title: Executive Order 11514—Protection and Enhancement of Environmental Quality

flags.justification: The Council on Environmental Quality is a multi-member body established by the underlying statute (NEPA), and agency-facing requirements (Sec. 2) are reasonably specific rather than open-ended.
Sec. 2(d) requires agencies to report compliance-review findings to CEQ by a specific deadline (September 1, 1970).
Multiple distinct agency responsibilities (monitoring, public information, data-sharing, statutory review) all coherently implement one statute, NEPA.
'Fullest practicable provision' and 'as appropriate' give agencies real discretion over implementation intensity without a firm floor.
No incentive concern distinct from the vagueness already captured under Flag 4.
Permanence carve-out applies: environmental protection is an ongoing, permanent government function, evergreen by nature.
This creates new evaluation, public-participation, and reporting requirements; no existing oversight is removed.

Sec. 2(b) requires 'the fullest practicable provision of timely public information... including provision for public hearings' — a real, structural public-participation mechanism providing genuine external input into agency environmental decisions.

This implements NEPA and establishes what became the central, still-operating federal environmental review apparatus (including the Environmental Impact Statement process), with the order's own text claiming a broad, ongoing, government-wide mandate.

CEQ's role is explicitly to coordinate existing agency functions, and Sec. 3(g) operates 'under the foreign policy guidance of the Secretary of State' for international matters — deferential, coordinated design.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 2/20 = 10.0%. Implements NEPA and establishes the Council on Environmental Quality's ongoing federal environmental review role — one of the most historically significant environmental-governance orders in the corpus, scoring low due to genuine public-participation and reporting safeguards.
